cataloging_subject.label: Speculum Vitae
cataloging_subject.scope_note: The cataloged subject is the anonymous Middle English Speculum Vitae: a 16,096-line catechetical poem in four-stress rhyming couplets, selectively translating and rearranging Laurent d'Orléans's Somme le roi and using the Latin Tabula de utilitate oracionis dominice. It excludes British Library Additional MS 33995 as a physical codex, Christine Robinson's transcription and thesis, the 1983 OTA deposit, U-M's 2023 structured encoding, Ralph Hanna's 2008 reading edition, and the General Corpus repository artifact. The historical attribution to William of Nassyngton is retained as disputed attribution history rather than authorship.

catalog_summary.title: Speculum Vitae
catalog_summary.author: Anonymous; formerly attributed to William of Nassyngton
catalog_summary.date: Third quarter of the fourteenth century (approximately 1350-1375); represented by a late-fourteenth-century Additional MS 33995 witness
catalog_summary.region: Yorkshire composition; the represented manuscript's first scribe is localized more narrowly to northwest Yorkshire, a witness-level dialect claim

notes: The conventional Latin title is used for discovery; the poem calls itself a Mirour of Lyf at lines 95-96 in the represented witness.

scope.description: Middle English self-reference in the represented witness

notes: Lines 95-96 say the lesson may be a Mirour of Lyf in which readers can see their whole life.

titles.value: Mirror of life (Middle English version translated from the Latin of the Speculum Vitae attributed to William of Nassington)

scope.description: Inherited bibliographic title in the 2023 CME header

notes: This source title preserves an obsolete William of Nassyngton attribution and an imprecise description of the source language. The main source work is Laurent d'Orléans's French Somme le roi, supplemented by Latin material.

titles.value: Almyghty Godde in Trynyte / In wham anely er persons thre

scope.description: Opening two lines of the Additional MS 33995 version represented by CME90022

titles.value: To whilk blisse he vs alle brynge / Yat on ye croyce for vs wald hynge

scope.description: Final two lines of the Additional MS 33995 version represented by CME90022

agents.name: Anonymous Middle English poet-translator of Speculum Vitae
notes: The collective type represents an unknown creator identity and does not imply multiple authors.

agents.name: William of Nassyngton
notes: Recorded only for disputed attribution history. Modern scholarship treats the naming passage in two non-central manuscripts as an interpolation and rejects his authorship of Speculum Vitae.

assertion.notes: The represented witness's conclusion asks prayer for the unnamed translator; it does not name William of Nassyngton.

scope.description: Selective Middle English verse translation from Laurent d'Orléans's French Somme le roi, supplemented by Latin material

assertion.notes: Translation is not mechanically line-for-line: the poet selects, rearranges, expands, and combines source material.

scope.description: Reorganization of Somme le roi around the Lord's Prayer and integration of Latin catechetical material

assertion.notes: The historical attribution existed, but Hanna's analysis as summarized by Newhauser rejects it because only two non-central manuscripts carry the naming interpolation and the alleged John Waldeby source is not the poem's source.

notes: Do not display William of Nassyngton as an accepted author.

date_statements.display: Third quarter of the fourteenth century (approximately 1350-1375)

assertion.notes: The broad third-quarter placement remains a useful scholarly consensus, but Glover's 2024 reassessment questions a Rolle-dependent terminus post quem used in Hanna's dating argument.

notes: The late-fourteenth-century production date of Additional MS 33995 is witness evidence, not a substitute for composition dating.
The OTA item's 1400-1499 field is not used as the work date.

places.label: Northwest Yorkshire
notes: Robinson's LALME-based localization of the represented manuscript's first scribe, not an exact composition site.

assertion.notes: Yorkshire is supported at regional scale; a more exact composition locality has not been established.

scope.description: Language of the first scribe in the represented Additional MS 33995 copy

assertion.notes: This is witness-level copying evidence and is not promoted to an exact authorial origin.

language_statements.label: Latin

scope.description: Lord's Prayer words, biblical and catechetical quotations, rubrics, headings, and other embedded Latin passages

notes: Latin is embedded within a primarily Middle English work; it does not make the poem mixed prose and verse.

form_statements.description: A 16,096-line poem in rhyming couplets, normally four-stress lines. The approved XML encodes individual L elements directly under divisions rather than grouping them in LG elements.

subject_statements.label: The Lord's Prayer and its seven petitions
notes: The Lord's Prayer supplies the organizing framework for the work rather than merely one local topic.

subject_statements.label: Christian doctrine and the Ten Commandments
The seven gifts of the Holy Spirit

summaries.value: Speculum Vitae is an anonymous Middle English catechetical poem of 16,096 four-stress lines in rhyming couplets. It organizes Christian teaching through the words and seven petitions of the Lord's Prayer, treating the Trinity, Decalogue, Creed, sacraments, virtues and vices, seven gifts of the Holy Spirit, beatitudes, and related moral instruction. Composed in Yorkshire in the third quarter of the fourteenth century, it selectively translates and rearranges Laurent d'Orléans's Somme le roi and incorporates material from the Latin Tabula de utilitate oracionis dominice. The represented Additional MS 33995 copy names no author; the former attribution to William of Nassyngton rests on an interpolation in other manuscripts and is rejected by modern scholarship.

After a 114-line prologue, the poem introduces the Pater Noster and its seven petitions, expounds the prayer's opening words through core catechetical systems, interprets each petition, and then coordinates the seven gifts with opposing sins and corresponding virtues before an 80-line conclusion.

extent.description: The approved XML represents one complete verse work in one DIV1 with five major DIV2 units, twenty DIV3 units, twenty DIV4 units, 65 HEAD elements, 16,096 L elements, 382 folio-column MILESTONE elements, four P elements, 43 Q elements, 989 HI elements, and eight CLOSER elements. The five major units contain 114, 128, 2,078, 13,696, and 80 lines respectively. These are representation metrics for the approved Additional MS 33995 transcription, not a claim that every manuscript or critical edition shares the same hierarchy or lineation.

content_parts.label: Prologue
content_parts.summary: Invocation and statement of the work's didactic purpose, including the self-description Mirour of Lyf.

content_parts.label: Pater Noster
content_parts.summary: Introduction to the Lord's Prayer and its seven petitions.

content_parts.label: First words of the Pater Noster
content_parts.summary: Exposition of Pater, Noster, Qui es, and In celis, incorporating the Trinity, Decalogue, Creed, sacraments, and seven virtues.

content_parts.label: Petitions of the Pater Noster and the Gifts
content_parts.summary: The seven petitions followed by the septenary scheme: the seven gifts of the Holy Spirit coordinated with sins, virtues, beatitudes, and moral instruction.

content_parts.label: Conclusion
content_parts.summary: Closing exhortation and prayer for the unnamed translator and audience, ending with a prayer for heavenly bliss through Christ's crucifixion.

related_works.title: Somme le roi
notes: Laurent d'Orléans's French prose Somme le roi is the principal source, but Speculum Vitae translates selectively, rearranges material, and imposes the Lord's Prayer as its organizing structure.

related_works.title: Tabula de utilitate oracionis dominice
notes: The initial approximately 3,510 lines also draw on this Latin catechetical source; no specific physical exemplar is identified here.

evidence.citation: CME90022 repaired-header XML in the pinned Kyvero-Press/CME submodule, commit e1d77c88ca561be924b2fedc54dd474be2e7e49c.
evidence.locator: HEADER; BODY/DIV1; five DIV2 units; DIV3 and DIV4 headings; L; MILESTONE; Q; HI; CLOSER
evidence.summary: Direct evidence for identifiers, inherited source title, poem language and form, incipit, self-title, explicit, complete text, subjects, five-part structure, lineation, folio mapping, and 2023 editorial frame.

evidence.citation: Reproducible structural and extent inspection of the approved CME90022 XML.
evidence.locator: Element counts, five major-unit line spans, first and last numbered lines, and folio-column milestones
evidence.summary: Confirms 845,280 bytes; one DIV1; five DIV2; twenty DIV3; twenty DIV4; 65 HEAD; 16,096 L; 382 MILESTONE; four P; 43 Q; 989 HI; and eight CLOSER elements. The five DIV2 units span lines 1-114, 115-242, 243-2320, 2321-16016, and 16017-16096 and map to fols. 1ra-1va, 1vb-2va, 2vb-14vb, 15ra-95vb, and 96ra-96rb.

evidence.citation: Christine Robinson, Speculum Vitae, Oxford Text Archive resource 0022.
evidence.locator: complete text, lines 1-16096 and folio-column markers 1ra-96rb
evidence.summary: Direct-source evidence for the represented Additional MS 33995 version's complete text, Middle English language, incipit, explicit, lineation, and folio-column locus. The separately cached header identifies Robinson, the witness, and the 1983 deposit.

evidence.citation: Christine Mary Robinson, ‘A machine-readable edition of the text of the Speculum Vitae as attested in British Library MS. Additional 33995’ (University of Edinburgh PhD thesis, title page 1987), volume 1.
evidence.locator: title and abstract; manuscript description and editorial preface; complete edition pp. 1-424, lines 1-16096
evidence.summary: Primary scholarly evidence for the represented witness, anonymous conclusion, manuscript locus, late-fourteenth-century date, northern and northwest-Yorkshire scribal evidence, complete text, verse extent, and transcription conventions.

evidence.citation: Ralph Hanna, ed., Speculum Vitae: A Reading Edition, 2 vols., EETS Original Series 331-332 (Oxford University Press, 2008).
evidence.locator: WorldCat bibliographic record and cached four-page title-page/contents preview
evidence.summary: Bibliographic and structural evidence for the current major reading edition. The title pages credit Ralph Hanna as editor using materials assembled by Venetia Somerset. The cached PDF is only front matter, so substantive claims from Hanna are cited through scholarly review rather than inferred from an unavailable full text; qpdf reports recoverable linearization hint warnings and the provider bytes remain unrepaired.

evidence.citation: Richard Newhauser, review of Ralph Hanna, Speculum Vitae: A Reading Edition, Journal of English and Germanic Philology 111, no. 1 (2012): 136–138, doi:10.5406/jenglgermphil.111.1.0136.
evidence.locator: discussion of authorship, date and place, verse form and extent, catechetical systems, and source works
evidence.summary: Supports anonymous authorship and rejection of the William attribution, Yorkshire composition in the third quarter of the fourteenth century, 16,096 four-stress lines in rhymed couplets, the Lord's Prayer as organizing frame, the five catechetical systems, selective translation and rearrangement of Somme le roi, and use of the Latin Tabula in the initial approximately 3,510 lines.

evidence.citation: Timothy Glover, ‘Late-Medieval Commonplace Culture, the Pastoral Compendium, and the Form of Richard Rolle’s The Form of Living,’ Review of English Studies 75, no. 319 (2024): 165–183, doi:10.1093/res/hgae007.
evidence.locator: discussion questioning direct dependence on Rolle's Form of Living and the resulting terminus post quem
evidence.summary: Recent scholarly evidence that a Rolle-based lower dating bound used for Speculum Vitae requires reconsideration. It does not establish a replacement exact date, so the third-quarter range remains medium-confidence rather than being silently discarded or overstated.

evidence.citation: British Museum, Catalogue of Additions to the Manuscripts in the British Museum in the Years MDCCCLXXXVIII-MDCCCXCIII (1894), 156-157.
evidence.summary: Historical catalogue evidence for the Additional MS 33995 work locus, late-fourteenth-century witness, unnamed conclusion in this copy, and inherited William of Nassyngton attribution contrasted with an interpolated naming passage in Royal 17 C VIII.

evidence.citation: Digital Index of Middle English Verse, version 1.0, record 423, IMEV/NIMEV 245.
evidence.locator: data/Records.xml record-423 and witness wit-423-26; IMEV/NIMEV 245; manuscript key BLAdd33995
evidence.summary: Versioned authority evidence for the conventional title, normalized incipit, aa couplet form, spiritual-advice subject, identifiers, forty-seven witness entries, two ghosts, and the represented Additional MS 33995 locus and boundary lines. DIMEV's retained William of Nassington attribution is cataloged as historical and is superseded for authorship by modern scholarship.

evidence.citation: General Corpus source-lineage manifest for CME90022.
evidence.summary: Linked graph evidence for the approved repository artifact, U-M/MEC encoding, 1983 OTA transcription, Robinson thesis, Hanna edition, Additional MS 33995, historical catalogue, cached complete sources, scoped rights, reproduction route, and unresolved facsimile and revision questions.

open_questions.question: How should Speculum Vitae's lower composition bound be revised if direct dependence on Richard Rolle's Form of Living is no longer accepted?
next_steps: Review Glover's complete 2024 argument together with Hanna's dating discussion and all independently dated early witnesses.
Separate source-based termini, manuscript-attestation termini, dialect evidence, and broad literary-historical dating.
Retain the third-quarter-fourteenth-century range at medium confidence until a replacement bound is grounded.

open_questions.question: Which recensions or physical exemplars of Somme le roi and the Tabula de utilitate oracionis dominice underlie Speculum Vitae, and what other source dependencies remain secure?
next_steps: Consult Hanna's complete source study and subsequent scholarship, not only the public front-matter preview and review.
Collate distinctive passages against candidate French and Latin recensions while distinguishing source works from surviving physical exemplars.
Reassess proposed Rolle parallels in light of Glover 2024.

open_questions.question: Why does DIMEV v1.0 contain forty-seven witness elements and two ghost references for record 423 while Newhauser describes Speculum Vitae as extant in forty-five copies?
next_steps: Compare Hanna's complete manuscript census with every DIMEV v1.0 witness and ghost entry.
Distinguish complete copies, substantial fragments, isolated excerpts, duplicate descriptions, lost or ghost witnesses, and newly identified material.
Report census counts with the source's counting rule rather than presenting forty-five or forty-seven as universally interchangeable.

open_questions.question: Can a complete color facsimile of Additional MS 33995 be obtained and mapped to Speculum Vitae with written reuse terms?
next_steps: Ask the British Library whether a complete preservation surrogate, legacy microfilm, or prior color image set exists.
If commissioning photography, request the whole codex and map the poem to ff. 1ra-96rb rather than requesting only cropped work pages.
Obtain separate written authorization or restrictions for private research, public Git distribution, scholarly publication, and commercial or noncommercial print-on-demand use.

open_questions.question: What textual and editorial differences separate the 1983 OTA deposit, the 1987 thesis edition, and U-M's 2023 structured representation?
next_steps: Collate OTA plain text against the thesis edition after controlling for PDF OCR and page layout.
Audit every italic span and silent expansion category against Robinson's conventions and, when available, manuscript images.
Document U-M-only hierarchy, lineation alignment, and selected manuscript headings separately from Robinson's transcription choices.

notes: The work is anonymous. William of Nassyngton is retained only as a disputed attributed author so discovery interfaces can explain, rather than repeat, the historical claim.
Yorkshire composition and northwest-Yorkshire scribal dialect are intentionally separate claims: the latter belongs to the represented witness's first scribe.
The 2023 CME hierarchy is a navigation and representation layer influenced by Hanna's reading edition; it is not presumed to reproduce the manuscript's visual hierarchy exactly.

The approved XML's 16,096 lines and five-part structure describe the Additional MS 33995 transcription. Newhauser reports forty-five surviving copies, while DIMEV v1.0 contains forty-seven witness elements and two ghosts; the differing census conventions remain unresolved, and abstract-work variation must not be collapsed into this one witness.

The repository's ignored source cache contains the complete OTA text and header, both complete Robinson thesis volumes, the complete 1894 British Museum catalogue, the complete DIMEV v1.0 data release, the four-page Hanna front-matter preview, and Glover's complete 38-page accepted manuscript. No public full manuscript facsimile was found as of 2026-07-14.
